dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a reported Stroke/CVA at --Address Redacted-- in the city of [CITY].
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Uh, he fell and became unresponsive. I don't think he's responding to anyone.
What are his main symptoms right now?
Well, he fell over and became confused without any warning.
Sir, can you open your eyes for me?
Mmm.
Can you tell me your name?
Hmm.
Press on our hands if you can.
Yeah.
Eyes: no response initially to verbal stimuli. Verbal: 3. Motor: 5. GCS is 11.
I am going to assess the airway. Dear sir, are you breathing as well as normal?
He's not breathing normally. I noticed something unusual.
Likely agonal and snoring respirations. We might have an airway issue.
I note airway sounds of snoring, which may indicate some blockage.
Airway needs closer inspection. Let's monitor just to make it clear for now.
Now I'm going to focus briefly on his breathing.
Breathing is at 26 per minute, but it seems agonal to me.
SpO2 reading is 54-it's low. Likely indicating poor oxygenation.
We should check his pulse now.
Radial pulse is 122 and is strong. No signs of shock so far.
Blood pressure is 171 over 96.
The blood glucose is 146. Not hypoglycemic based on this.
On a scale of 0-10, do you think he's had pain lately?
Not that I can tell. He just fell and seemed confused-never mentioned pain.
Bystander, can you go over what you were doing when the incident started?
We were picking up cans all morning. Then he suddenly fell.
When he fell, was there anything strange you noticed before he collapsed?
No. Suddenly dropped. I panicked and called 911 after administering some Narcan.
He seems to fit the Opioid Overdose Protocol (7-1) based on his overdose symptoms and signs.
Let me assess your pupils now. I'm going to shine a light in them.
Yeah.
Pupils are 2mm bilaterally and sluggish. No signs of unusual movement or trauma.
Let me listen to your lungs briefly. You comforted with that?
Mm.
Both lungs are clear and equal bilaterally. Chest is stable for now.
Test your arms by pressing against these palms.
Mmm.
Equal strength and coordination on both arms.
Press the palms against these for the test legs.
Yes.
Equal strength in the legs, regular motor coordination noted.
Can you point out any bumps or sores in body that suggest trauma?
He did take a fall, but I can't see any marks or abrasions.
I don't see any DCAP/BTLS.
EKG reading is sinus tachycardia which is consistent with opioid overdose.
We are now initiating 4 Lead ECG monitoring.
Setting up naloxone for next administrations now.
Administering 0.5mg naloxone IV now per standing orders for suspected opioid overdose.
Now Breathing is 19, heart rate is 135. SpO2 at 99, BP is 136 over 87, more stable.
Preparing patient for stretcher transport now.
Let's put him on the stretcher with semi-Fowler's position for maximum comfort.
Shoulder and lap belts attached for transport safety.
Transporting him to [COMPANY_NAME] as per protocol.
Continuous ECG monitoring while en route. That's been initiated.
Treatment included IV insertion and naloxone for opioid overdose symptoms identified.
GCS improved to 13 after Narcan, still sedated but intermittently rousable.
Vitals two minutes post-naloxone: Pulse 97, SpO2 97, BP 135 over 87.
Response is still slurred when roused, and the patient is showing signs of sedation post-naloxone.
Airway is patent after naloxone, so no further interventions are required for the moment.
Blood sugar remains elevated at 146, but the patient shows no hypoglycemic signs.
The patient is still rousable but lacks full orientation, and strength remains equal bilaterally.
We will continue monitoring the patient en route for any changes in behavior or airway status.
Unable to obtain past medical history due to intermittent responsiveness.
Bystander reported the patient had been out collecting cans in the morning, but we have no specific details on food or drink intake.
No additional medications given at this time due to questionable substance use.
EKG remains sinus tachycardia with no indication of acute changes.
Bystander added that after being given naloxone, the patient mentioned he "did some drugs," but he is not specific about the type.
Transport under continues to be justified for further diagnostic evaluation and monitoring.
Transport conditions remain stable, and the patient has maintained a patent airway.
Further evaluation by hospital ER is necessary to rule out neurological or toxological outcomes.
No known loss of motor or sensory function and peripheral pulses remain strong.
Respiratory effort and symmetrical chest movement are improving post-naloxone.
Pain score remains 0 throughout the evaluation despite drug use concern.
Patient remains in semi-Fowler's with regular breathing and minimal verbal interaction.
Respiratory rate dropped from 26 to 11; more regular and effort appears better than before.
We will continue monitoring the patient en route for any changes in behavior or airway status.
Skin is dry and normal in color after prior diaphoresis, with no signs of rash or bruising.
Alveolar breathing sounds are now equal bilaterally post-naloxone.
No signs of neck stiffness or abnormal head posture are observed en route.
All procedures successfully completed and confirmed by Partner for transport.
Pulse and BP continue to trend in a stable direction post-naloxone administration.
No medications could be confirmed as it is unclear if the patient brings his own drugs at all.
Blood sugar remains elevated at 146, but the patient shows no hypoglycemic signs.
No complaints or signs of rectal bleeding or abnormal urination noted in the patient.
EKG is consistent with sinus tach and no arrhythmias due for now.
Vital sign trends and response to naloxone have been clearly communicated to the ER staff.
Final call completed, and protocols followed through to hospital transfer.
Arrival at [COMPANY_NAME] imminent for further neurological and tox screen assessment.
